name: Cáceres: Prince's Park and Historic Quarter Loop
name_original: Cáceres – city tour circular por el casco histórico
description: Explore Cáceres's UNESCO World Heritage historic quarter on this easy 4.5-mile (7.2 km) hiking tour, passing palaces and the Co-Cathedral.
region_intro: Hiking around Cáceres offers diverse landscapes, from the dramatic river gorges of Monfragüe National Park to the varied terrain of the Sierra de Gredos. The region features lush forests of chestnut and cork oaks, unique granite rock formations, and numerous rivers and reservoirs. Hikers can explore gentle hills, challenging mountain paths, and well-preserved historical routes. Best hiking trails around Cáceres The most popular hiking route is Cáceres – city tour circular by the historic quarter, a 4.5 miles (7.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 56 minutes to complete. This easy route explores the city's historic quarter. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cáceres Main Square – Torre de Bujaco loop from Cáceres, a difficult 3.2 miles (5.2 km) path. This route connects the city's Main Square with the historic Torre de Bujaco. Local hikers also love the View of the Landscape – Shelter With a View loop from Estadio Príncipe Felipe, an 11.3 miles (18.2 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 4 hours 55 minutes. Hiking around Cáceres is defined by lush forests, granite rock formations, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy city walks to moderate and difficult trails. The routes in Cáceres are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 190 reviews. More than 1100 hikers have used komoot to explore Cáceres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Cáceres?
A: Cáceres offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 110 routes documented on komoot. These range from easy strolls through historic towns to challenging mountain ascents, ensuring options for every skill level.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Cáceres?
A: The climate in Cáceres is generally favorable for hiking for much of the year. However, &lt;strong&gt;spring and autumn&lt;/strong&gt; are particularly pleasant due to milder temperatures, making them ideal seasons to explore the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes in Cáceres?
A: Hiking in Cáceres offers incredibly diverse terrain. You'll find everything from the dramatic river gorges and granite rock formations of Monfragüe National Park to the varied landscapes of the Sierra de Gredos, featuring lush forests of chestnut and cork oaks. Many routes also follow river valleys and historic paths.

Q: Are there easy hiking options suitable for beginners or families in Cáceres?
A: Yes, Cáceres has plenty of easy hiking options. Approximately 50 of the routes are classified as easy. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990829940/caceres-city-tour-circular-by-the-historic-quarter" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres – city tour circular by the historic quarter&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.5-mile route perfect for a leisurely exploration of the city's historical center. The Los Barruecos Natural Monument also offers easy circular walks.

Q: Where can I find more challenging hikes in the Cáceres region?
A: For those seeking a challenge, Cáceres offers about 22 difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17316357" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres Main Square – Torre de Bujaco loop from Cáceres&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 3.2-mile path connecting the city's main square with the historic Torre de Bujaco. The Sierra de Gredos also provides more demanding mountain ascents.

Q: What natural attractions can I see while hiking in Cáceres?
A: Cáceres is rich in natural beauty. You can explore the unique granite rock formations at &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6093807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos Natural Monument&lt;/a&gt;, observe griffon vultures at Monfragüe National Park, or discover the hidden &lt;a href="https://visitambroz.es/en/senderismo-caceres/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chorrera de Hervás waterfall&lt;/a&gt; in the Valle del Ambroz. Many trails also feature scenic rivers and reservoirs like the Embalse de José Maria Oriol.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural points of interest along the trails?
A: Absolutely. Cáceres is steeped in history. Many routes pass through historic villages, offering insights into Roman and Jewish heritage. Within the city, you can explore the historic quarter, including landmarks like the Torre de Bujaco. Long-distance trails like the Ruta Vía de la Plata (Silver Route) and the Ruta de Isabel la Católica also offer a deep dive into the region's cultural past.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Cáceres?
A: The komoot community highly rates hiking in Cáceres, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 190 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the region's diverse landscapes, from lush forests to unique rock formations, and the well-marked trails that make navigation easy. The rich biodiversity, including opportunities for birdwatching, is also a common highlight.

Q: Are there many circular hiking routes in Cáceres?
A: Yes, many of the hiking routes in Cáceres are circular, offering convenient starting and ending points. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990829940/caceres-city-tour-circular-by-the-historic-quarter" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres – city tour circular by the historic quarter&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17316357" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres Main Square – Torre de Bujaco loop&lt;/a&gt;. The Los Barruecos Natural Monument is also known for its accessible circular walks.

Q: Which hikes offer the best viewpoints in Cáceres?
A: For stunning panoramic views, consider routes in Monfragüe National Park, particularly near Monfragüe Castle, which offers unique perspectives of the Tagus River and the famous 'Salto del Gitano' (Vulture Rock). The Sierra de Gredos also provides multiple viewpoints over the Jerte and Ambroz valleys. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31447220" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Landscape – Shelter With a View loop&lt;/a&gt; is another great option for scenic vistas.

Q: Are hiking trails in Cáceres dog-friendly?
A: While specific regulations can vary by protected area, many natural trails in Cáceres are generally dog-friendly, especially if dogs are kept on a leash. It's always advisable to check local park rules, particularly in areas like Monfragüe National Park, which is a protected bird sanctuary. Always ensure your dog is under control and that you clean up after them.

Q: Are there any hikes that feature waterfalls in Cáceres?
A: Yes, the Valle del Ambroz region is home to the beautiful Chorrera de Hervás, a notable waterfall. Hikes in this area, such as those mentioned on &lt;a href="https://visitambroz.es/en/rutas-senderismo-caceres/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;visitambroz.es&lt;/a&gt;, can lead you to this picturesque natural feature.

Q: How accessible are the hiking trails in Cáceres by public transport or car?
A: Many trails, especially those closer to Cáceres city, are accessible by car, with parking often available near trailheads or in nearby villages. For routes further afield, a car is generally recommended for convenience. While some larger towns may have bus connections, public transport options directly to more remote trailheads can be limited, so planning ahead is advised.
tips: Very integrated in the city, it is located between Avenida Hernán Cortés and Avenida Rodríguez de Ledesma. It is a pleasant space in which we find a large central pond and an Open Air Sculpture Museum, which we can contemplate while walking through it. At the main entrance there is a botanical garden and in the final section, a fountain known as La Madrila, restored by the Escuela Taller, used, at the time, for a watering hole and laundry.

Located in the Plaza de San Juan, this church is one of the most populous parishes in the city. It was formerly called San Juan de los Ovejeros, because the transhumant cattle ranchers who visited the city settled in the area when the cattle fairs were held.
The church is from the 13th century but with profound reforms in the 14th, 15th and 17th centuries. In the sixties of the 20th century, the church was remodeled to restore its original style, eliminating the altarpiece and subsequent decorations.

It is presided over by the Town Hall built in 1869 by Ignacio María de Michelena. In it you can see such emblematic monuments as the Torre de la Hierba, the Torre de los Púlpitos and especially the Torre de Bujaco next to the Hermitage of Peace.

The Holy Church Co-Cathedral of Santa María is the most important Christian temple in Cáceres and, since 1957, it has the rank of Co-Cathedral.
The origin of this church dates back to the 13th century as a Romanesque temple, but its destruction led to the creation of a new late-Gothic church between the 15th and 16th centuries. Late-Romanesque corbels - heads of the beam that protrude outside - remain from this Romanesque period on the western and southern Gothic doors.

In this church, the images it houses stand out above all, the most important being the Nazareth Jesus, carved in Seville in 1609 by Tomás de la Huerta and San Judas Tadeo of great devotion in the city.

Located in the Palacio de las Veletas, it conserves a magnificent cistern inside.
Inaugurated in February 1933, the Cáceres Museum occupies two palace-houses in the Monumental City: the Palacio de las Veletas, which houses the archeology and ethnography sections, and the Casa de los Caballos, which houses the collection of Fine Arts from 1992.
The Parador de Cáceres is housed in a 14th-century stone palace and features original features such as vaulted ceilings and period furniture.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Cáceres city tour circular by the historic quarter?
faqs.answer: This city tour is classified as easy, making it suitable for all fitness and skill levels. It doesn't require special equipment, though comfortable walking shoes are recommended due to the historical paving and some inclines.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this tour?
faqs.answer: The terrain within the historic quarter features a mix of wide squares and narrow alleys. While generally manageable, be aware that the Old Jewish Quarter, which is part of the route, includes distinct slopes and steep streets.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll be immersed in the UNESCO World Heritage site of Cáceres. Key sights include the Arco de la Estrella, the 12th-century Torre de Bujaco, and Plaza de Santa María with the Co-Cathedral of Santa María, Cáceres. Other notable points are the Cáceres Main Square, the Church of St John the Baptist, the Church of Santiago, and the Cáceres Museum and Cistern. You'll also pass by the Palace of the Marquises of Torreorgaz and the charming Old Jewish Quarter.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this city tour?
faqs.answer: The tour typically takes around 1 hour and 56 minutes to complete, but you might want to allow extra time to explore the historic sites and enjoy the atmosphere.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to do this city tour?
faqs.answer: Cáceres is beautiful year-round, but spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) generally offer the most pleasant weather for walking, avoiding the peak heat of summer.

faqs.question: Is this city tour dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the historic quarter is an open public space, specific rules for dogs may apply in certain buildings or indoor attractions. It's generally fine to walk your dog on a leash through the streets and squares, but always be mindful of local regulations and other visitors.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this tour?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or general entrance fees required to walk this circular city tour through the historic quarter of Cáceres. However, individual attractions like museums or churches along the route may have their own admission fees.

faqs.question: Where can I park or access the trailhead for this tour?
faqs.answer: The tour is a circular route through the historic quarter, so you can start at various points. For parking, consider areas just outside the monumental zone. Public transport options are available to reach the city center, which is the starting point for exploring the historic quarter.

faqs.question: What should I bring or wear for this city tour?
faqs.answer: Comfortable walking shoes are essential due to the historical paving and some inclines. Depending on the season, bring water, sun protection (hat, sunscreen) for warmer days, or layers for cooler weather. A camera is also a great idea to capture the stunning architecture.

faqs.question: Is the tour accessible for people with mobility challenges?
faqs.answer: While much of the historic quarter features wide squares, some parts, particularly the Old Jewish Quarter, have distinct slopes and steep, cobbled streets. This might present challenges for those with limited mobility or using wheelchairs. It's advisable to check specific routes if accessibility is a primary concern.

faqs.question: Is this city tour suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Yes, this easy city tour is generally suitable for families. Children can enjoy exploring the ancient streets and imagining the history. However, be mindful of the duration and some inclines, especially for very young children, and plan for breaks if needed.
